text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a hemion hernia. It's a appendicitis within an inguinal hernia.

This is a elderly gentleman with a lot of medical problems on anticoagulation who had developed appendicitis a few weeks earlier and was treated with antibiotics by his physicians due to risk of surgery.
He's been optimized a bit and he was really complaining of tremendous groin pain and he just didn't think that he could wait.
On exam, you could feel an irreducible mass in the groin and CAT scan did confirm that it was the appendix.
Because of his ongoing symptoms and him being better optimized, it was recommended that he go to the operating room for an appendectomy.
The surgeon observes adhesions between the cecum and appendix within the inguinal hernia sac.
Did not appear that this was a sliding hernia more so that this was adherence of the appendicitis into the indirect space.
The surgeon grasps the mesoappendix and pulls it out, dividing the visible attachments to the surrounding tissue.
I was not planning on doing a hernia repair at the time of this operation. This was more or less just to get the appendix out.
I chose an MIS operation because I knew that it would take me fifteen minutes or so and avoid dragging the appendix through the groin if we were to have done this in an open fashion.
I have subsequently repaired this gentleman's inguinal hernia. I did an open lichtenstein under sedation.
Their medical comorbidities really were prohibitive and We wanted to avoid a second general anesthesia.
The surgeon has mostly extracted the appendix from the indirect space and is carefully dissecting additional adhesions to clearly identify the appendiceal mesentery.
The surgeon attempts to create a window in the tissue to facilitate the placement of a ligature around the appendiceal mesentery, which is positioned away from the surgeon's view.
The surgeon creates a window in the tissue and slides the ligature through it to resect the remaining meso-appendix.
The surgeon addresses residual adhesions around the appendix, clearing the area to prepare a clean base for ligation or amputation.
The surgeon places an endo loop at the base of the appendix, opting to avoid using staplers for this appendectomy.
And then the second one, you could just as easily do a free tie or create your own version of an endo loop.
The surgeon divides the appendix at its base, completing the separation from the meso-appendix.
The surgeon cauterizes the exposed mucosa of the appendix stump. The stump is left as it is without imbrication.

The patient did have a rocky course, as expected from the anesthesia and his comorbidities, but eventually did well enough that I, like I mentioned, I fixed his inguinal hernia, opened Lichtenstein under local omen. I hope you enjoyed.
